description: The image displays a circular publisher interface badge labeled 'Check for updates' with a central red bookmark icon, containing no discernible scientific micrographs, diffraction patterns, spectra, mechanical curves, or quantitative data relevant to the studied material. No caption, coordinate axes, legends, scale bars, or sample identifiers are visible, so no direct visual verification of microstructural features is possible. The broader paper context reports an experimental investigation of an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel, comparing uncharged and hydrogen-charged (101 mass ppm) specimens using ECCI, EBSD, STEM, and tensile testing to evaluate hydrogen-enhanced microbanding and hydrogen embrittlement resistance. Reported results include dislocation cell structures, GNB misorientation increases, deformation band propagation, and ductile dimple fracture refinement; however, none of these details are observable in the provided pixel content. Consequently, the figure-level scientific information is unreadable and cannot serve as direct visual evidence for the deformation mechanisms or property trends discussed in the text.

Figure 1 presents the thermal desorption analysis (TDA) curve showing the hydrogen desorption rate (mass ppm/min) as a function of temperature (300–1000 K) for the hydrogen-charged Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C austenitic low-density steel. The sample was exposed to a pressurized gaseous hydrogen environment of 100 MPa at 543 K for 200 h prior to testing according to the experimental description, and the TDA measurement was conducted at a heating rate of 100 K/h using gas chromatography with a thermal conductivity detector. The curve displays a single, well-defined desorption peak centered near 700 K with a maximum desorption rate of approximately 0.58 mass ppm/min; citation states that this peak corresponds to lattice diffusion. The total measured hydrogen concentration integrated from this profile is 101 mass ppm as reported in the caption, whereas citation reports the estimated concentration in the uncharged sample is below 5 mass ppm. The absence of additional peaks suggests negligible strong hydrogen trapping at structural defects. This figure provides critical experimental evidence that the hydrogen content is uniformly distributed and remains effectively constant during subsequent mechanical testing, establishing the essential hydrogen precondition for evaluating its effect on deformation microbanding and hydrogen embrittlement resistance.

This figure presents the room-temperature tensile behavior of an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel comparing uncharged and hydrogen-charged (101 mass ppm) conditions tested at 5.0×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹. Panel (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves where the hydrogen-charged sample (blue) displays slightly higher flow stresses and marginally greater ductility than the uncharged sample (red), with both exceeding fracture strains of 0.5. Panel (b) plots the corresponding true stress-strain curves (solid lines) alongside strain-hardening rate curves (dotted lines), which are nearly superimposable for both conditions. The strain-hardening curves exhibit four characteristic stages labeled A through D: an initial sharp drop (Stage A), a brief constant-rate plateau (Stage B), a gradual increase to a peak near true strain ~0.15 (Stage C), and a monotonic decrease toward fracture (Stage D). As stated in the caption, no additional twinning-related hardening stage emerges upon hydrogen charging. This similarity in strain-hardening response indicates that hydrogen does not activate new deformation twinning mechanisms, supporting the core conclusion that the steel's hydrogen embrittlement resistance arises from hydrogen-enhanced microbanding rather than twinning-induced plasticity.

Figure 3 presents inverse pole figures (IPFs) along the tensile axis (TA) direction for an austenitic FeMnAlC low-density steel, comparing grain orientations in the uncharged (a) and hydrogen-charged (b) samples after uniform tensile deformation to ε = 0.4. Each data point represents an evaluated grain orientation (approximately 13–14 per condition), color-coded by dislocation structure category: Type 1a (black) and Type 1b (gray) cell-block structures delimited by geometrically necessary boundaries (GNBs) and incidental dislocation boundaries (IDBs), Type 2 (red) dislocation cells, and Type 3 (blue) complex structures. The triangular IPFs are anchored by the key deformation texture components <0 0 1>//TA, <1 1 1>//TA, and the [1 1 2]–[1 0 1]//TA crystallographic line, with specific grain orientations annotated by Miller indices such as [6 3 14], [11 3 13], [5 2 10], and [7 2 9]. The comparison reveals a redistribution of dislocation structure types across crystallographic orientations upon hydrogen charging (101 mass ppm), supporting the conclusion that hydrogen modifies the grain-orientation dependence of deformation microstructures and slip activity in this austenitic steel.

Figure 4 presents electron microscopy images classifying the dislocation structures in the uncharged austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel after room-temperature tensile deformation. Panels (a) and (b) show the Type 1a structure: (a) is a lower-magnification micrograph (scale bar 1 μm) displaying elongated cell blocks delineated by geometrically necessary boundaries GNB1 and GNB2 aligned close to {111} traces, with the tensile axis (TA) indicated; (b) provides a higher-magnification view (200 nm scale bar) revealing incidental dislocation boundaries (IDBs) subdividing the cell blocks between GNB1. Panels (c) and (d) depict the Type 1b structure: (c) shows two intersecting GNB sets (GNB1 and GNB2) at lower magnification (1 μm); (d) highlights the GNB2s boundaries between GNB1 at higher magnification (500 nm). The comparison demonstrates orientation-dependent dislocation patterning, where Type 1a exhibits one dominant GNB family with internal IDBs, whereas Type 1b displays two active GNB systems. This baseline classification of cell-block morphologies in the uncharged material is essential for subsequent comparison with hydrogen-enhanced deformation structures.

Figure 5 presents ECCI micrographs classifying the dislocation structures in the uncharged austenitic FeMnAlC low-density steel. Subfigures (a) and (b) show the Type 2 dislocation structure: (a) provides a lower-magnification overview with a 1 μm scale bar and the tensile axis (TA) indicated, while (b) resolves dislocation cells (DCs) at higher magnification (200 nm scale bar). Subfigures (c–e) depict the Type 3 complex dislocation structure, characterized by a homogeneous distribution of DCs together with inhomogeneous microbands (MBs) and dense dislocation walls (DDWs); (c) additionally reveals deformation twins (DTs) and MBs aligned along highly stressed slip planes, whereas (d) and (e) offer higher-magnification views of DCs and plate-type MBs with a width range of 150–200 nm, respectively. The caption states that Type 2 forms in grains oriented close to [0 0 1]//TA directions where octahedral slip operates, whereas Type 3 forms near [1 1 1]//TA where slip is dominated by three slip systems on two non-coplanar planes. This figure establishes the baseline deformation architecture for subsequent quantitative analysis of hydrogen-induced changes in dislocation structure type and boundary misorientation.

This figure presents combined ECCI and EBSD analysis of geometrically necessary boundary (GNB) and incidental dislocation boundary (IDB) misorientations in Type 1a cell blocks (CBs) of an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel. Panel (a) is an ECC image showing CB1 and CB2 with a 1 μm scale bar and a {111} orientation marker. Panel (b) is the corresponding grain reference orientation deviation EBSD map (reference point at the lowest kernel average misorientation) with a 0–7° color scale, revealing local point-to-point deviations such as 0.78°, 1.52°, and 2.80° across the CBs. Panel (c) is a higher-magnification ECC image (200 nm scale bar) resolving IDB1 through IDB5 within CB1, bounded by GNB1. Panel (d) is an EBSD point-to-point misorientation profile along the interior of CB1, quantifying IDB misorientations of 1.0° (IDB1), 0.85° (IDB2), 0.5° (IDB3), 0.7° (IDB4), and 0.6° (IDB5). The figure establishes the boundary identification and measurement protocol; the caption and text state that hydrogen does not modify Type 1a CB morphology, and this dataset underpins subsequent statistical findings that hydrogen raises average GNB misorientation by approximately 1° while causing smaller changes in IDB misorientation.

This Figure 7 comprises four histogram panels quantifying the influence of hydrogen charging on boundary misorientation distributions in an austenitic FeMnAlC low-density steel. Panels (a) and (b) analyze Type 1a cell blocks, showing GNB1 and IDB misorientation fractions, respectively, for uncharged (GNB1-uncharged/IDB-uncharged, pink) and hydrogen-charged (GNB1-charged/IDB-charged, blue) conditions. Panels (c) and (d) analyze Type 1b cell blocks, displaying GNB1 and GNB2 misorientation distributions under identical conditions. The average misorientation values are explicitly marked: in Type 1a, θ̄_GNB1 increases from 1.62° to 2.70° and θ̄_IDB from 1.14° to 1.38° upon hydrogen charging; in Type 1b, θ̄_GNB1 increases from 2.64° to 3.65° and θ̄_GNB2 from 2.67° to 3.71°. All distributions for charged samples shift toward higher misorientation angles relative to uncharged counterparts, with GNBs exhibiting larger hydrogen-induced increases than IDBs. These quantitative results support the mechanism of hydrogen-enhanced microbanding via GNB-assisted microband nucleation, where hydrogen promotes boundary splitting through increased geometrically necessary dislocation storage and localized shear, leading to greater lattice rotations along pre-existing dislocation boundaries.

Figure 8 presents a correlative ECCI and KAM-EBSD analysis of geometrically necessary boundary (GNB) structures in a deformed austenitic FeMnAlC low-density steel. Panels (a) and (b) show ECC images of a Type 1a cell block (CB) formed by a single GNB system (GNB1), where panel (b) resolves dense dislocation walls (DDWs) and lenticular microbands (MB1a, MB1b) with measured thicknesses of 88 nm, 150 nm, and 190 nm, respectively. Panel (c) provides the corresponding KAM-EBSD map quantifying local boundary misorientations of θ^H_DDW1a = 1.0 ± 0.1°, θ^H_MB1a = 1.9 ± 0.1°, and θ^H_MB1b = 2.0 ± 0.2°, establishing that GNB misorientations below 2° correspond to DDWs while those above 2° are associated with microbands. Panel (d) is an ECC image of a Type 1b CB revealing localized formation of MB1a and MB1b along GNB1 sections alongside a second GNB set (GNB2). These results demonstrate quantitative criteria to distinguish DDWs from MBs based on boundary misorientation and reveal localized microband formation along GNB segments.

Figure 9 comprises a BF-STEM image (a) and its corresponding diffraction pattern (b) from hydrogen-charged Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C austenitic low-density steel. Panel (a), captured under a two-beam condition using a (111)-type diffraction vector with a 200 nm scale bar, reveals a microband (MB) nucleated along the primary geometrically necessary boundary (GNB) system within a Type 1 cell block, in a grain oriented close to [10 3 16] parallel to the tensile axis (TA). The MB manifests as a paired dislocation sheet structure aligned parallel to the primary GNB system (1 -1 1). Panel (b) presents the selected-area diffraction pattern along the [1 0 1] zone axis (scale bar 20 1/nm), exhibiting fundamental fcc reflections such as 111, 200, 022, and -111. No extra diffraction spots are visible along <200> and <220> directions, which the authors interpret as absence of ordered kappa-carbides. These observations directly support the conclusion that hydrogen promotes localized MB nucleation along GNBs, underpinning the hydrogen-enhanced microbanding mechanism in this alloy.

This figure integrates IPF-EBSD and ECCI to reveal hydrogen-enhanced Type 2 dislocation structures in an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel tensile-tested at room temperature. Panel (a), an IPF-EBSD map (10 μm scale bar), shows macroscopic deformation bands (DB1, DB2, DB3) partitioning a grain oriented close to [1 1 18]//TA, with boundaries perpendicular to the primary slip direction. Panel (b) zooms into DB1 (5 μm scale bar) near the [2 1 15]//TA orientation, revealing crystallographic microbands (MBs) consistent with {111}<110> slip traces. Panels (c–e) are ECCI images at increasing magnifications (1 μm, 500 nm, and 250 nm) resolving the DB1 interior: (c) shows bright MB plates (~150–200 nm thick) aligned along {111} planes and spaced ~2–3 μm; (d) resolves a single MB adjacent to dislocation cells (DCs); and (e) details equiaxed DCs. Together, these demonstrate that hydrogen promotes multiscale strain localization—macroscopic DBs (10–30 μm) and microscopic MBs/DCs—shearing the grain interior.

Figure 11 displays the statistical distribution of dislocation cell size (D_DC) for type 2 dislocation structures in an austenitic FeMnAlC low-density steel, comparing uncharged (red curve) and hydrogen-charged (blue curve, approximately 101 mass ppm H) conditions based on quantitative ECC image analysis. The x-axis represents cell size from 100 to 300 nm and the y-axis shows the fraction. The uncharged specimen exhibits a relatively broad distribution with a peak around 220 to 230 nm and an average D_DC of 220 plus or minus 10 nm. In contrast, the hydrogen-charged specimen displays a sharper, left-shifted distribution peaking near 200 nm with a refined average D_DC of 192 plus or minus 10 nm. This comparison reveals that hydrogen reduces the dislocation cell size and narrows the size distribution, indicating hydrogen-induced refinement of the dislocation substructure. These quantitative ECCI-based measurements support the broader conclusion that hydrogen enhances microband formation by promoting finer dislocation cells, which is proposed as a key mechanistic feature contributing to the superior hydrogen embrittlement resistance of this steel.

This figure presents four ECCI micrographs showing the hydrogen-influenced Type 3 dislocation structure in an austenitic FeMnAlC low-density steel, recorded under (2 0 2) channeling conditions. The caption states the observed grain is oriented close to [5 4 6] parallel to the tensile axis (TA). Panel (a) displays a well-developed dense dislocation wall (DDWs) structure containing dislocation cells (DCs), with the caption reporting that DCs possess an average size of 210 ± 10 nm. Panel (b) shows deformation bands (DBs) propagating through the pre-existing DDW/DC structure. Panel (c) reveals DBs together with closely spaced arrays of in-grain microband (MB) plates cutting through localized regions. Panel (d) provides a higher-magnification detail of an individual MB imaged as a bright plate. According to the caption and associated text, DBs and MBs align close to (-1 1 1) plane traces via secondary slip activation, and the average MB thickness is similar to that of the DC structure, about 200 nm. The figure collectively demonstrates that hydrogen promotes the formation of DDWs, DBs, and MBs while having a negligible role in the DC structure, supporting a DB-assisted MB nucleation mechanism that contributes to mechanical stabilization.

This figure presents BF-STEM images of Type 3 dislocation structures in an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel, comparing uncharged (a) and hydrogen-charged (b) samples after tensile deformation at 295 K. Subfigure (a) captures deformation bands (DB1–DB3) and dense dislocation walls (DDWs, dashed lines) within a grain oriented close to [6 5 8]//TA, whereas (b) shows coarser DBs traversing DDWs in a grain near [9 7 12]//TA. Images were taken under a two-beam condition using a (1 1 1)-type diffraction vector. Arrows indicate sheared DDW segments at DB-DDW intersections. Analysis reports that the hydrogen-charged specimen develops significantly larger shear offsets (~75–100 nm for DB3) compared with negligible offsets in the uncharged sample, and that the DBs are thicker (~0.5–1.0 μm versus ~0.3–0.5 μm) with individual bands merging into coarser structures. These findings illustrate hydrogen-enhanced strain localization and slip-band coarsening via secondary slip system (-1 1 1) [1 0 1] activity, supporting hydrogen-enhanced microbanding in austenitic steels.

Figure 14 presents BF-STEM micrographs and corresponding diffraction data from a hydrogen-charged austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel tensile tested at 295 K. Panel (a) is a bright-field STEM image taken under two-beam conditions with a (111)-type diffraction vector, showing a deformation band (DB) shearing through a pre-existing dense dislocation wall (DDW) and dislocation cell (DC) structure in a grain oriented near [9 7 12] parallel to the tensile axis (TA). White arrows indicate sheared DC walls offset by the DB, while dashed lines demarcate the DDW and DB boundaries; the scale bar is 250 nm. Panel (b) provides the selected-area electron diffraction pattern along the [1 0 1] zone axis with a scale of 20 1/nm, indexing fundamental fcc reflections such as (111), (200), and (022) without extra spots along <200> or <220>. This absence of additional diffraction spots indicates that strain localization within the DBs originates from a hydrogen-induced effect on dislocation plasticity rather than a phase transformation or ordering. Together, the images support that hydrogen enhances microbanding by promoting intense dislocation activity and shearing of the dislocation structure.

Figure 15 presents four BF-STEM micrographs captured under two-beam conditions with a (111)-type diffraction vector, revealing the nanoscale dislocation architecture inside deformation bands (DBs) of hydrogen-charged austenitic FeMnAlC low-density steel. Panels (a) and (b) show a high density of long screw and mixed dislocations (~1.3×10^14 m^-2) within DBs, with annotations identifying groups of closely spaced screw dislocations (A), dense dislocation walls (B), paired screw dislocation configurations (C), and dissolved DDW segments (D). Panel (c) reveals numerous small cusps identified as jogs (J) on screw and mixed dislocations, which act as pinning points for single-ended dislocation sources, alongside cross-slipped dislocations (CS). Panel (d) provides a crystallographic analysis of a double-cross slipped dislocation (CS), tracing a (-1 1 1)[1 0 1] screw dislocation that cross-slips onto a (-1 -1 1) plane and then back onto another (-1 1 1) plane, with [0 1 1] and [1 0 1] directions marked. Collectively, these images demonstrate that hydrogen-enhanced plasticity within DBs involves massive secondary slip shearing the pre-existing DDW/DC structure, extensive cross-slip and double-cross-slip enabling screw dislocations to propagate through DDW barriers, and jog-mediated dislocation multiplication, supporting a DB-assisted microband nucleation mechanism.

This figure presents comparative distributions of cell boundary misorientation and cell size for Type 3 dislocation structures in an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel, comparing uncharged and hydrogen-charged (101 mass ppm H) conditions tested at room temperature. Subfigure (a) shows the misorientation distribution (θ_DC) obtained from EBSD data, where the uncharged sample exhibits an average misorientation of 0.87° and the hydrogen-charged sample shows 0.90°, indicating only a marginal increase upon hydrogen charging. Subfigure (b) displays the cell size distribution (D_DC) estimated from ECC images, revealing that hydrogen charging significantly refines the average cell size from 236 ± 10 nm to 212 ± 10 nm. The fraction curves demonstrate that the size distribution peak shifts toward smaller dimensions under hydrogen, whereas the misorientation distribution remains largely unchanged. These observations support the core conclusion that hydrogen enhances microbanding primarily by refining dislocation cells without substantially altering boundary misorientations, consistent with a hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB) mechanism that promotes strain localization and plastic accommodation.

This figure presents SEM fractographs of tensile fracture surfaces from an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel, comparing uncharged (a-c) and hydrogen-charged (d-f) samples tested at 295 K and an initial strain rate of 5.0×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹. Panels (a) and (d) provide low-magnification overviews with 100 μm scale bars, while (b, c) and (e, f) are magnified images with 20 μm scale bars taken from the rectangular frames marked in (a) and (d), respectively. Both conditions exhibit fully ductile dimple fracture; however, the hydrogen-charged sample displays noticeably finer dimple sizes. The caption and text state that hydrogen refines dimple size from approximately 2–8 μm to 1.5–3.0 μm through void sheets produced by hydrogen-enhanced strain localization. Importantly, no grain boundary cracking is observed in the charged sample, indicating that hydrogen does not alter the ductile fracture mechanism. The figure supports the core conclusion that hydrogen-enhanced microbanding relaxes the internal stress fields of deformation bands, thereby suppressing grain boundary damage mechanisms and contributing to the high hydrogen embrittlement resistance of this steel.

This schematic illustration (Figure 18) depicts two distinct hydrogen-induced microband (MB) nucleation mechanisms operative in an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel under tensile deformation at 295 K with 101 mass ppm hydrogen. Panel (a) illustrates the GNB-assisted MB nucleation mechanism, where hydrogen promotes splitting of geometrically necessary boundary (GNB) structures through enhanced storage of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs), increasing local boundary misorientation (caption reports θ ~ 2°–8° in hydrogen-charged vs. ~2°–3° in uncharged samples) and nucleating localized MBs along pre-existing cell-block GNBs and incidental dislocation boundaries (IDBs). Panel (b) shows the DB-assisted mechanism, wherein hydrogen-induced strain localization within deformation bands (DBs) activates massive secondary slip that shears the primary dense dislocation wall (DDW) / dislocation cell (DC) structure; subsequent cross-slip, dislocation annihilation, and Lomer-Cottrell lock formation mechanically stabilize the MBs inside the grain interior. The figure supports the core conclusion that these complementary hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB) mechanisms accommodate localized shear while the DB-assisted pathway specifically relaxes the internal stress field of DBs, thereby suppressing grain-boundary cracking and contributing to the high hydrogen embrittlement resistance of the steel.

This figure presents a scatter chart plotting the index of hydrogen embrittlement (IHE) versus ultimate tensile strength (UTS) for several austenitic steels and multicomponent face-centered-cubic alloys tested under gaseous hydrogen charging conditions. The horizontal axis displays IHE from 0.7 to -0.2, the vertical axis shows UTS in MPa from 400 to 1100, and a top color gradient bar visually ranks HE resistance from low (red) to high (blue). The caption reports that the plot is divided into three threshold regions: low resistance (IHE > 0.1), moderate (0.1 > IHE > 0), and high (IHE < 0). The legend identifies four fracture surface characteristics: small-sized dimple/dimple fracture (SD/D, blue), ductile and intergranular fracture (D+IG, light blue), hydrogen-induced successive slip or quasi-cleavage (HIS, orange), and intergranular/transgranular plus quasi-cleavage (IG/TG + QC, red). Alloys with low HE resistance, such as FeMnC TWIP steels and 304/316 stainless steels, exhibit high positive IHE values (~0.15 to 0.6) and are linked to brittle IG/TG+QC or HIS modes. By contrast, high-resistance materials—including CoNiV, HP160, 310S, CoCrFeMnNi, FeNiCrCo, 316L, and 317L—cluster at IHE ≤ 0 within an elliptical highlight and predominantly show SD/D or D+IG fracture. The present Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel is marked by a distinctive starburst symbol at approximately IHE = -0.05 and UTS ≈ 585 MPa, placing it in the high-resistance region. The caption states that this positioning, obtained with a hydrogen content of ~101 mass ppm, confirms the present steel's exceptional HE resistance comparable to top-performing alloys, attributing it to hydrogen-enhanced microbanding and related plasticity accommodation mechanisms.
